source_title: Why We Migrated from Kubernetes to Serverless — And Cut Our Cloud Bill by 60%

analysis.title: Why We Migrated from Kubernetes to Serverless — And Cut Our Cloud Bill by 60%
analysis.core_message: A mid-stage SaaS company reduced infrastructure costs by 60% and developer overhead by migrating from self-managed Kubernetes to serverless architecture.
key_points: K8s operational overhead consumed 40% of one engineer's time
Cloud bill dropped from $12K/mo to $4.8K/mo within 3 months
Cold starts were a concern but solved with provisioned concurrency
Team velocity increased — 2x faster feature shipping
analysis.target_audience: SaaS CTOs, DevOps engineers, startup founders managing cloud costs
hooks: Our K8s cluster was costing us $144K/year — and most of it was waste.
We thought we needed Kubernetes. We were wrong.
60% cost reduction. 2x shipping speed. One migration.
stats_or_quotes: 40% of one engineer's time on K8s ops

content.linkedin_post:
We just cut our cloud bill by 60%.

Not by negotiating. Not by downsizing.

By killing our Kubernetes cluster.

Here's what happened:
→ $12K/mo on K8s (most of it wasted on idle capacity)
→ 40% of one engineer's time babysitting configs
→ Scaling? Manual. Painful. Slow.

So we migrated to serverless.

Results after 3 months:
✅ Cloud bill: $4.8K/mo (down from $12K)
✅ Zero ops overhead — no more YAML nightmares
✅ 2x faster feature shipping
✅ Auto-scaling that actually scales

The cold start concern? Solved with provisioned concurrency.

Kubernetes is powerful. But for a 30-person SaaS company?
It was like driving a semi truck to the grocery store.

Sometimes the best infrastructure decision is using less of it.

#DevOps #Serverless #CloudCosts #SaaS #StartupOps
twitter_thread:
We cut our cloud bill by 60% by killing Kubernetes.

Thread on what we learned 🧵
1/ Our K8s setup cost $12K/mo. Most clusters were over-provisioned. We were paying for capacity we never used.
2/ One engineer spent 40% of their time on K8s ops. Helm charts, config drift, node scaling, networking issues. That's not shipping product.
3/ We migrated to serverless (Lambda + API Gateway + DynamoDB). Took 6 weeks. The scariest part? Cold starts. We solved it with provisioned concurrency — $200/mo extra, still massive savings.

4/ Results after 90 days:
- $12K → $4.8K/mo cloud spend
- Zero ops overhead
- 2x faster deploys
- Auto-scaling that actually works

5/ K8s is incredible for companies at scale. But for most startups under 50 people? You're over-engineering. Ship product, not YAML.

Full write-up on our blog.
content.email_newsletter:
Subject: We cut our cloud bill by 60% (here's how)

Hey [First Name],

Quick story from the trenches.

Our Kubernetes cluster was costing us $12K/month. One engineer was spending nearly half their time on ops. We were scaling manually and over-provisioning everything.

So we did something that felt radical: we killed it.

Migrated to serverless in 6 weeks. Here's what happened:

• Cloud bill dropped to $4.8K/mo
• Zero ops overhead (no more config drift nightmares)
• Feature shipping speed doubled
• Auto-scaling that just works

The catch everyone worries about — cold starts — was solved with provisioned concurrency. Cost us an extra $200/mo. Worth every penny.

If you're running K8s at a startup under 50 people, it might be worth asking: is this complexity actually helping us ship faster?

For us, the answer was no.

Full breakdown on the blog → [link]

Cheers,
[Team]

We migrated from Kubernetes to serverless and here's what changed:

💰 $12K/mo → $4.8K/mo
⚡ 2x faster deploys
🛠️ Zero ops overhead
📈 Auto-scaling that works

Kubernetes is powerful — but sometimes simpler wins.

#CloudComputing #DevOps #Serverless #StartupLife #SaaS #TechOps #CloudCosts
content.summary_blurb: A mid-stage SaaS company shares their journey from self-managed Kubernetes to serverless, achieving a 60% cloud cost reduction ($12K to $4.8K/month), eliminating DevOps overhead, and doubling feature shipping speed — with practical advice on handling cold starts and when K8s might be overkill.

suggestions: Could add a CTA in the LinkedIn post
